name: Gio Hải
subtitle: A long, flat quartz-sand strand on the South China Sea coast of central Vietnam, six kilometres south of the former Cửa Việt Combat Base and just below the parallel that once divided North and South Vietnam. Typhoon coast: forty-eight tropical cyclones have passed within a hundred kilometres of here since 1980.
nearest_city_blurb: Gio Linh is the district seat, inland and west of the beach. Its territory sits immediately south of the former Demilitarised Zone along the Bến Hải River.
intro: Gio Hải sits on a long quiet stretch of the central-Vietnamese coast, about six kilometres south of the old river-mouth at Cửa Việt. The beach is not on the domestic-tourist itinerary — most visitors to Quảng Trị come for the war sites (the Bến Hải bridge, the Vịnh Mốc tunnels, Khe Sanh, the Cửa Việt base) rather than the shore. It is a working coast: fishing boats pulled up at the tide line, nets folded on the sand, and very little else. The shallow slope makes it good for a long walk in either direction; the forty-eight cyclones on record within a hundred kilometres since 1980 are the reason the buildings are set well back from the water.

setting.paragraph: Quảng Trị province forms the narrowest stretch of Vietnam — mountains of the Annamite Range press in from Laos to within about 75 km of the sea. The Bến Hải River, 15 km north of this beach, was the demarcation line between North and South Vietnam from the 1954 Geneva Accords until reunification in 1975; the Demilitarised Zone ran along its north and south banks. The coast itself is a continuous strand broken by river mouths — Cửa Việt, Cửa Tùng, Cửa Hàm — each one a small fishing harbour and, in the war, a U.S. Navy or Marine Corps logistical position. The sand is quartz-dominant, washed down from the erosion of the Annamite ranges; the climate is tropical monsoon, with a vast autumn rainy season (four to five hundred millimetres a month from September through November) and a dry, hot, southwest-monsoon summer.

nearby_features.name: Cửa Việt Base
nearby_features.blurb: Former U.S. Marine Corps, U.S. Navy, U.S. Army and ARVN combat base at the mouth of the Cửa Việt river. A logistical hub for the northern I Corps during the Vietnam War.

nearby_features.name: Battle of Cửa Việt
nearby_features.blurb: Fought 25–31 January 1973, at the Cửa Việt naval base — one of the last combined-arms actions of the Vietnam War, overlapping with the Paris Peace Accords ceasefire.

nearby_features.name: Cửa Việt river mouth
nearby_features.blurb: Outlet of the Thạch Hãn / Cửa Việt river system into the South China Sea. Fishing harbour on the south bank; small resort development on the north.

nearby_features.name: Mai Xá (Gio Mai) village
nearby_features.blurb: A Gio Mai-commune village inland; traditional Quảng Trị rural architecture and a small temple complex.

nearby_features.name: Hiền Lương Bridge / former DMZ
nearby_features.blurb: The bridge over the Bến Hải River that marked the dividing line between North and South Vietnam, 1954–1975. National historical site; museum on the south bank.

nearby_features.name: Gio Linh district seat
nearby_features.blurb: District capital. Markets, the district seal's Giao bưu (postal-communications) memorial, and the buses onward to Đông Hà.

context_photos.caption: Cửa Việt Beach, 4 km north of Gio Hải — the nearest named beach along this coast, at the river mouth.
Morning on Cửa Việt beach — same strand, photographed by a later visitor.
Rural landscape at Cửa Việt township, Gio Linh district — Lunar New Year, 2018.
The Hiền Lương Bridge over the Bến Hải River — the former line between North and South Vietnam, 15 km north-northwest of the beach.

context_photos_note: Gio Hải itself has not been photographed for Wikimedia Commons. These images are from the adjoining Cửa Việt beach and coast (4 km north), and from the national-historical site at the former DMZ crossing (15 km north-northwest).

climate.water_temp_source_note: Water-temp curve is a typical South-China-Sea coastal monthly profile placeholder pending the sea-surface-temperature pipeline.

predicted.regime_plain: Quartz 60 % · feldspar 3 % · rock fragments 38 %. A quartz-dominant mix washed down from the Annamite range to the west, with a substantial lithic fraction reflecting the volcanic and metamorphic rocks of the interior uplands. The low feldspar content is consistent with a hot, wet, chemical-weathering climate that breaks feldspar down long before it reaches the coast.

storm_history.paragraph: Forty-eight named tropical cyclones have tracked within a hundred kilometres of here since 1980 — roughly one every thirteen months. The strongest reached Category 3. The typhoon season runs broadly from August into November; it is the reason the September–November rainfall total at Gio Hải is four hundred and sixty, five hundred and thirty, and four hundred and seventy millimetres. The coast is exposed, the water shallow, and there is no protective headland — local buildings are set well back behind a low dune.

registry_status.note: Not listed by Vietnam National Administration of Tourism (vietnamtourism.gov.vn) or on Quảng Trị provincial tourism pages. The named-beach destinations along this coast are Cửa Việt (4 km north) and Cửa Tùng (15 km north).

summary.character: Mixed — usually swimmable, with a livelier season
